title: Boiled lemon drops
subtitle: Hard lemon sweets cooked to hard crack stage
excerpt: Traditional boiled sweets made from sugar and glucose syrup cooked to hard crack, flavoured with lemon juice, lemon zest, and citric acid for sharpness. Poured onto a lined tray, cooled, and broken into pieces. The result is clear, intensely flavoured hard candy.
sourceNotes: Boiled sweets are one of the oldest forms of sugar confectionery, with hard sugar candy appearing in British and European records from at least the seventeenth century. The lemon drop format, combining cooked sugar with citrus flavouring and citric acid, is a standard British boiled sweet tradition. The glucose syrup prevents crystallisation, which is the modern home confectionery improvement over earlier methods that relied on cream of tartar.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Boiled sweets keep for several weeks in an airtight tin lined with baking paper. Do not store in the fridge; condensation makes them sticky. Separate layers with baking paper.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven; sugar syrup cooked to hard crack stage (155 degrees C) on the hob

glossaryTerms.definition: Hard candy cools and sets very quickly once poured. From the moment it leaves the heat to the moment it is fully solid is roughly 10 minutes at room temperature. All equipment must be ready: the lined tray, any flavourings pre-measured, and the area cleared. Once poured, the candy cannot be reheated without losing clarity. If it sets before you have finished spreading, it can be broken rather than cut; it will still be correct.

content.text: Boiled sweets require respect for the

after the sugar comes off the heat. Set up everything before you start cooking. A sugar thermometer is not optional: hard crack is a precise temperature range, and estimating by colour alone leads to soft, sticky sweets.

Line a baking tray with baking paper. Combine 300 g granulated sugar, 100 g glucose syrup, and 100 ml water in a heavy-based saucepan. Stir over medium heat until the sugar dissolves, then stop stirring. Bring to a boil and cook without stirring until the syrup reaches 155 degrees C on a sugar thermometer. Swirl the pan gently if the colour is uneven, but do not stir.

Remove from the heat. Carefully add the juice and zest of 1 lemon, 0.5 tsp citric acid, and the food colouring if using. The mixture will spit briefly. Stir once to combine, then pour immediately onto the lined tray. Tilt the tray to spread the mixture thinly. Leave completely undisturbed until cold and solid, about 45 minutes. Break into pieces by dropping the tray onto a work surface or tapping firmly with the back of a spoon. Dust with a little icing sugar if pieces are sticky.

items.problem: Candy is sticky and does not harden
items.solution: The syrup did not reach 155 degrees C. Soft, sticky candy means the temperature was too low. A sugar thermometer calibrated against boiling water is a useful check before you start.

items.problem: Syrup turned an opaque white rather than clear
items.solution: The syrup crystallised. This happens if the mixture was stirred after boiling, if crystals dropped from the pan sides, or if humidity is very high. Crystallised candy cannot be rescued. Prevent it by brushing the pan sides with a wet pastry brush in the early stages of cooking.
